aliases: Jeera Rice / Zeera Rice / Cumin Rice

english: Jeera Rice / Zeera Rice / Cumin Rice
romanized: Jeera Rice / Zeera Rice / Cumin Rice

food_subgroup: Cooked basmati rice tempered with cumin and ghee

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Jeera Rice. Nearest parent is a cooked rice dish with spice tempering (FOODON:03311737 — cooked rice dish). A new concept for Indian cumin-tempered rice preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked rice — closest parent used; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for cumin-tempered basmati rice preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A03SL (cooked rice) is the closest defensible parent. No specific facet code for cumin tempering exists in the current FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Jeera Rice as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Jeera Rice is approximately 200 g (one bowl), providing around 324 kcal, 7 g protein, 6.4 g fat, and 63 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Basmati Rice and Ghee Composition Data
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) using component data for cooked basmati rice and ghee in proportions consistent with standard Jeera Rice preparation (approximately 90% cooked basmati rice, 5% ghee, 5% cumin and spices by weight of finished dish). Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (basmati rice, cooked; ghee) where ICMR-NIN data were not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Basmati rice, white, boiled (Atkinson et al.); University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked basmati rice
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white basmati rice. Jeera Rice is approximately 90% cooked basmati rice by weight; the ghee and cumin tempering contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and are expected to marginally reduce the glycaemic response compared to plain boiled basmati rice. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 31.5 g per 100g × 200g serving / 100) / 100 = (58 × 63) / 100 = 36.5 rounded to 36.5 for 200g serving. Note: GL per 100g portion = (58 × 31.5) / 100 = 18.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~145 mg per 100 g; ~290 mg per 200 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Jeera Rice contains approximately 290 mg sodium per standard 200 g bowl, which is within acceptable limits for a hypertensive diet; however, individuals with severe hypertension should monitor total daily sodium intake when consuming Jeera Rice alongside high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted curries.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycaemic index (~58) and moderate glycaemic load (~36.5 per 200 g bowl)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Basmati rice has a meaningfully lower GI (~58) than standard white rice (~73), and the addition of ghee further slows gastric emptying, making Jeera Rice a preferable rice choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; nonetheless, portion control remains essential as the glycaemic load per 200 g bowl is approximately 36.5, which is classified as high.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.8 g per 100 g) and negligible trans fat; cholesterol 7.5 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Jeera Rice prepared with a modest amount of ghee provides only 1.8 g saturated fat and 7.5 mg cholesterol per 100 g, which is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet when consumed in standard portions; cumin seeds also contribute antioxidant compounds that may support cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cumin seeds contain anti-inflammatory and antioxidant phytochemicals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cumin seeds used in Jeera Rice contain thymoquinone and other antioxidant compounds with documented anti-inflammatory properties, and ghee provides butyrate and fat-soluble vitamins, making this dish generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jeera Rice is a classic, aromatic North Indian rice preparation made from long-grain aged basmati rice cooked by the absorption method to produce individual, separate, fluffy grains, then finished with a quick tempering (tadka) of whole cumin seeds (jeera) crackled in hot ghee until they release their characteristic earthy, nutty fragrance. Optional whole spices such as bay leaves, cloves, and green cardamom are sometimes added during tempering for additional aromatic depth. The finished dish has a delicate, subtly spiced flavour with a light buttery richness from the ghee and a distinctive cumin aroma. It is the most common restaurant alternative to plain steamed rice in North Indian cuisine and is traditionally served alongside Dal Tadka, Rajma, Butter Chicken, or rich meat curries. Nutritionally, a 200 g bowl provides approximately 324 kcal, 7 g protein, 6.4 g fat, and 63 g carbohydrate. Basmati rice has a lower glycaemic index (~58) than standard white rice (~73), and the presence of ghee further moderates the glycaemic response, making Jeera Rice a relatively better choice for blood glucose management compared to plain white rice. The dish is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, and is suitable for most dietary patterns when consumed in appropriate portions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN component data for cooked basmati rice and ghee rather than a direct laboratory analysis of Jeera Rice as a finished dish. The proportions used (approximately 90% cooked basmati rice, 5% ghee, 5% cumin and spices) are consistent with standard restaurant preparation but may vary. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked white basmati rice as the closest approved analogous food.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Jeera Rice as a finished dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026; these values would require laboratory analysis or detailed fatty acid data for the specific ghee used. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the finished Jeera Rice dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN component tables consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA data for basmati rice and ghee combination. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; basmati rice and ghee contain no vitamin C. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived B12 source (ghee contains negligible B12). GL per 200g serving = 36.5 (high GL); GL per 100g = 18.3 (medium). Copper (0.1 mg), manganese (0.5 mg), and vitamin K (0.5 mcg) values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as supplementary data where ICMR-NIN did not report these micronutrients. FoodEx2 code A03SL used as closest parent for cooked rice; no specific code for cumin-tempered basmati rice exists in the current FoodEx2 catalogue. The input record listed glycemic_load as 36.5 for a 200g serving; this has been retained as the per-serving GL and 18.3 recorded as the per-100g GL in gi_source notes.
